paying social media personalities people trust to fake excitement for a product is not good for society.

paragraphs.text: I agree with all the proposed steps to strengthen consumer rights in the online space. There is way to many predatory tactics being used against consumers. Not everyone have the capacity to make rational decisions when being bombarded with every possible manipulative tactic all at once. Preying on the consumers fear of missing out with count downs and content that is only available for a limited time, using gambling mechanics that belongs in casinos and paying social media personalities people trust to fake excitement for a product is not good for society. We must not forget that many that are being exposed to these tactics are kids and young adults who do not have the capacity to understand that their brain is being programmed. It's depressing to see kids developing gambling addictions through digital product. We should also not forget that according to the EU-commision around 17% of all adults in the union have some sort of mental disability (I'm talking about things like depression, problems with angst, ADHD, Bipolar disorder and autism). Culture and media is something that is extremely important for people to get through their everyday lives and bring joy, relaxation and excitement. In my opinion predatory tactics only erode our culture while bringing nothing of value to the table. In addition to this I believe we need to take a look at how the right to own digital goods is handled. If I for example buy stuff online (like a movie or a video game) I should NOT get a license that can be revoked in any way. If I pay for something I should own it. I used to be able to have a DVD collection with all my stuff, but nowadays many things don't come to DVD. That's fine but if it's only on a streaming service stuff I pay for can get revoked and that's not OK. It should be mandatory to supply the consumer with a file that can be downloaded and saved for the future if the service goes down. This is also very important for the preservation of our culture and arts. Libraries don't contain digital goods in the amount that is necessary for our culture not to be controlled by private companies. I also have hundreds of games on "Steam" (which is the biggest provider of digital games), but when I die my collection will be rendered useless. Even if I explicitly want to transfer my licenses to my son and put that in my will, he won't have access to the collection I've been building over the last 20 years because in the TOS Steam explicitly states that the licenses cannot be transferred under any circumstance. This is total insanity. I can inherent my fathers vinyl collection. I inherited my grandmother's book collection. Digital goods should also be able to change owners. The push to make everything digital needs to be regulated so that the citizens of the EU aren't being ripped of. My suggestion is that companies that supply digital goods should need to provide ways of transferring licenses to between the consumers if they request it. It was totally fine for decades when goods where physical and companies still made lots of money and everything was fine and dandy. Companies should also be forced to provide ways for the user to continue using the product even if the service that provided it shuts down for some reason. To summarize: If someone buys something, they should own it. Right now it's pretty ridiculous because the consumer have no real rights to their product whatsoever because of the strange "you buy a license, not a product" clause that's in every TOS
